raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "The Senate Committee on Energy, Utilities and Communications passed SB 1374 to restore the ability for utility customers with on-site solar generation to be credited for self-consuming their electricity.",
    "Solar energy generated over a school's parking lot and separately metered from the electricity used by classrooms is currently considered 'exported' and compensated at a low price.",
    "SB 1374 will ensure that schools are fairly compensated by netting out generation and consumption occurring during the same time period across multiple meters.",
    "California cannot meet its carbon emission reduction goals without properly supporting its schools in the transition to clean energy.",
    "Solar energy and battery storage are critical for protecting students' learning time and meeting California's clean energy and climate adaptation goals.",
    "Public schools have a general fund that covers utility bills among other expenses, and rising electricity bills can divert funds from hiring support staff and providing essential services.",
    "In previous years, solar energy helped offset rising costs for public schools in California, allowing them to invest savings in educational equity.",
    "SB 1374 requires the California Public Utilities Commission to update its tariffs for multiple meters to give credit for self-consumption like that of single-family homes.",
    "Restoring and protecting incentives for solar energy installations is critical for ensuring that all students receive a quality education.",
    "SB 1374 will allow apartments, schools, community colleges, universities, and other entities to save on energy costs by installing solar and energy storage."
  ]
}
```
